lessons.title: Mortal and Venial Sin (Capital and Lesser Sins)
lessons.contentHTML:
Definition

Sin is an offense against God and a rejection of His love. Mortal sin destroys the life of grace in the soul, while venial sin wounds it. Capital sins are the root vices from which other sins arise. Lesser sins are those that do not break our relationship with God but still damage it (CCC 1846–1876).

Scriptural References

  • 1 John 5:16–17 – "There is sin which is mortal… All wrongdoing is sin, but there is sin which is not mortal."
  • Romans 6:23 – "The wages of sin is death, but the free gift of God is eternal life in Christ Jesus our Lord."
  • Galatians 5:19–21 – The works of the flesh, which prevent inheriting the kingdom of God.
  • James 1:14–15 – Sin, when fully grown, brings forth death.

Catechism References

  • CCC 1849 – Sin is an offense against reason, truth, and right conscience; it is failure in genuine love for God and neighbor.
  • CCC 1854 – Sins are evaluated according to their gravity: mortal or venial.
  • CCC 1855 – Mortal sin destroys charity; venial sin allows it to remain but offends and wounds it.
  • CCC 1857 – Mortal sin requires grave matter, full knowledge, and deliberate consent.
  • CCC 1866 – The capital sins are pride, greed, envy, wrath, lust, gluttony, and sloth.

Proclamation

"Sin divides us from God, but His mercy is greater — through repentance and grace we are restored to life."

Explanation

Mortal sin is a grave violation of God’s law that destroys the divine life (sanctifying grace) in the soul. For a sin to be mortal, three conditions must be met: the act must be of grave matter, committed with full knowledge, and with deliberate consent. Mortal sin, if unrepented, leads to eternal separation from God.

Venial sin is a lesser offense against God’s law that wounds charity but does not break our relationship with God. Venial sins dispose us little by little toward greater sin if left unchecked.

Capital sins are the root causes of many other sins and inclinations to sin. These include: pride, greed, envy, wrath, lust, gluttony, and sloth. They are called "capital" because they give rise to other sins and vices.

Lesser sins refer to venial sins and minor faults, which, while not destroying grace, can weaken the soul and make it more vulnerable to serious sin.

Christ calls us to continual conversion, rooting out both mortal and venial sin through prayer, examination of conscience, the sacraments, and the cultivation of virtue.

lessons.title: Catholic Social Teaching
lessons.contentHTML:
Definition

Catholic Social Teaching (CST) is the Church’s body of doctrine on human dignity, solidarity, and the common good, rooted in Scripture and Tradition. It offers moral principles for building a just society and living a life of holiness in our relationships with others and the world (Compendium of the Social Doctrine of the Church, CCC 2419–2463).

Scriptural References

  • Micah 6:8 – "Do justice, love kindness, and walk humbly with your God."
  • Matthew 25:40 – "Whatever you did for one of the least of these my brethren, you did for me."
  • Acts 4:32 – "The company of those who believed were of one heart and soul… they had everything in common."
  • James 2:15–16 – Faith without works of mercy is dead.

Catechism References

  • CCC 2419 – CST is built on the dignity of the human person and the pursuit of the common good.
  • CCC 2420 – The Church gives moral judgment on economic and social matters when required by faith and the moral law.
  • CCC 2422 – The Church’s teaching in this area is a catechesis in truth and love.
  • CCC 2443 – God blesses those who come to the aid of the poor and rebukes those who turn away from them.
  • CCC 2458 – CST applies the Gospel to the structures of society.

Proclamation

"Catholic Social Teaching calls us to see Christ in every person and to work for a world where human dignity is upheld and the common good is served."

Explanation

Catholic Social Teaching emerges from the Church’s reflection on the Gospel in light of human experience. It is a call to live the love of Christ in public life and to transform society in justice and charity.

The key principles of CST include:

  • Life and Dignity of the Human Person – Every human being is created in the image of God and must be respected from conception to natural death.
  • Call to Family, Community, and Participation – The family is the basic unit of society, and all are called to participate in the common good.
  • Rights and Responsibilities – Human rights come with the responsibility to protect the rights of others.
  • Option for the Poor and Vulnerable – We must prioritize the needs of the poor and marginalized.
  • Dignity of Work and Rights of Workers – Work is a form of participation in God’s creation, and workers deserve just wages and safe conditions.
  • Solidarity – We are one human family, called to pursue peace and justice together.
  • Care for God’s Creation – We are stewards of the earth, responsible for protecting it for future generations.

CST is not optional for Catholics — it is an essential expression of our faith in action. It applies to personal life, parish life, politics, economics, and global relations.

lessons.title: Mercy and Justice
lessons.contentHTML:
Definition

Justice is the moral virtue that gives God and neighbor their due. Mercy is the loving compassion that goes beyond justice, offering forgiveness, relief, and help even when it is not strictly owed (CCC 1807, 2447).

Scriptural References

  • Micah 6:8 – "Do justice, love mercy, and walk humbly with your God."
  • Matthew 23:23 – Jesus teaches that justice, mercy, and faithfulness are weightier matters of the law.
  • James 2:13 – "Mercy triumphs over judgment."
  • Luke 6:36 – "Be merciful, even as your Father is merciful."

Catechism References

  • CCC 1807 – Justice consists in the firm and constant will to give their due to God and neighbor.
  • CCC 1931 – Respect for the human person demands the recognition of human rights and duties.
  • CCC 2447 – Works of mercy are charitable actions by which we help our neighbor in body and soul.
  • CCC 2840 – Forgiveness is a high point of Christian prayer, a witness to mercy.
  • CCC 2448 – In its various forms, mercy is expressed especially toward the poor and suffering.

Proclamation

"Justice gives each their due; mercy gives beyond what is due — for God’s mercy overflows where justice ends."

Explanation

Justice and mercy are not opposed but work together in God’s plan. Justice ensures fairness, order, and the right relationships between persons and with God. Mercy perfects justice by offering love, forgiveness, and help to those in need.

In human society, justice sets the standard for what is owed; mercy moves us to go beyond that standard in love. Jesus embodies both perfectly — fulfilling the demands of justice through His sacrifice while pouring out mercy on sinners.

The Christian is called to live both virtues: to uphold justice in all dealings and to practice mercy in forgiving offenses, aiding the suffering, and showing compassion without discrimination.

Mercy without justice can enable wrongdoing; justice without mercy can become harsh and unforgiving. Together, they reflect the fullness of God’s love and call us to restore both right relationships and hearts.

lessons.title: Introduction to Prayer
lessons.contentHTML:
Definition

Prayer is the raising of one’s mind and heart to God or the requesting of good things from Him. It is the living relationship of the children of God with their Father, with His Son Jesus Christ, and with the Holy Spirit (CCC 2559–2565).

Scriptural References

  • Philippians 4:6 – "Do not be anxious about anything, but in everything by prayer and supplication with thanksgiving let your requests be made known to God."
  • Matthew 6:6 – "When you pray, go into your room and shut the door and pray to your Father who is in secret."
  • Luke 11:1 – "Lord, teach us to pray."
  • Psalm 145:18 – "The Lord is near to all who call on Him, to all who call on Him in truth."

Catechism References

  • CCC 2559 – Prayer is the raising of one's mind and heart to God.
  • CCC 2560 – God thirsts that we may thirst for Him.
  • CCC 2564 – Christian prayer is a covenant relationship between God and man in Christ.
  • CCC 2565 – Prayer is the living relationship of the children of God with the Holy Trinity.
  • CCC 2590 – The prayer of the Church unites us with Christ’s own prayer to the Father.

Proclamation

"Prayer is the heartbeat of the Christian life — our continual conversation with the God who loves us."

Explanation

Prayer is more than speaking words to God; it is communion with Him. It is an expression of love, trust, and dependence on our Creator. Through prayer, we adore God, confess our sins, express gratitude, and bring our needs before Him.

Christian prayer takes many forms: vocal prayer, meditation, and contemplation. Each deepens our relationship with God in a unique way.

Prayer is both a personal and communal act. Personally, it is our one-on-one time with the Lord; communally, it unites us with the Church in worship, especially in the liturgy.

God initiates the desire for prayer, and we respond. This dialogue changes us, conforms us to His will, and draws us into His life of grace. Prayer is essential for spiritual growth, discernment, and perseverance in faith.

lessons.title: The Lord's Prayer - Our Model
lessons.contentHTML:
Definition

The Lord’s Prayer, taught by Jesus Himself, is the perfect model of Christian prayer. It sums up the Gospel, expresses our relationship with God, and teaches us how to pray with trust, humility, and love (CCC 2759–2865).

Scriptural References

  • Matthew 6:9–13 – Jesus teaches the Lord’s Prayer in the Sermon on the Mount.
  • Luke 11:2–4 – The Lord’s Prayer given in response to the disciples’ request, “Lord, teach us to pray.”
  • Romans 8:15 – "You have received the Spirit of adoption, by whom we cry, ‘Abba! Father!’"
  • Psalm 103:13 – "As a father has compassion for his children, so the Lord has compassion for those who fear him."

Catechism References

  • CCC 2759 – The Lord’s Prayer is the summary of the whole Gospel.
  • CCC 2761 – It is truly the prayer of the Church, given by the Lord to His disciples.
  • CCC 2777 – Calling God “Father” expresses a childlike relationship with Him.
  • CCC 2822 – The Father’s will is that “all men be saved” and come to the knowledge of the truth.
  • CCC 2845 – Forgiveness is at the heart of Christian prayer, modeled in the Lord’s Prayer.

Proclamation

"When we pray the Lord’s Prayer, we speak with the very words of Jesus, uniting our hearts to His."

Explanation

The Lord’s Prayer is unique because it comes directly from Jesus, who commands, “Pray then like this.” It begins with adoration, calling God our Father, acknowledging His holiness, and desiring His Kingdom.

It then turns to petitions: for God’s will to be done, for our daily bread, for forgiveness of sins, and for deliverance from evil. These petitions teach us dependence on God for both spiritual and material needs.

Each phrase is rich in meaning:

  • Our Father – We are God’s children, united as one family.
  • Hallowed be Thy name – We praise and honor God’s holiness.
  • Thy kingdom come – We long for God’s reign of justice, peace, and love.
  • Thy will be done – We submit to God’s plan with trust.
  • Give us this day our daily bread – We ask for all that we need, especially the Eucharist.
  • Forgive us our trespasses – We seek mercy and extend it to others.
  • Lead us not into temptation – We ask for protection from sin.
  • Deliver us from evil – We pray for victory over Satan and all harm.

The Lord’s Prayer is both a personal and communal prayer, expressing our trust in God and our unity with the whole Church.

lessons.title: Traditions and Types of Prayer
lessons.contentHTML:
Definition

The Church’s tradition recognizes various forms and expressions of prayer, all inspired by the Holy Spirit, which draw us into deeper communion with God. These include vocal prayer, meditation, and contemplation, as well as the traditional categories of adoration, contrition, thanksgiving, and supplication (CCC 2626–2643, 2700–2724).

Scriptural References

  • Philippians 4:6 – "In everything by prayer and supplication with thanksgiving let your requests be made known to God."
  • Psalm 95:6 – "O come, let us worship and bow down, let us kneel before the Lord, our Maker!"
  • 1 Thessalonians 5:16–18 – "Rejoice always, pray without ceasing, give thanks in all circumstances."
  • Matthew 26:39 – Jesus’ prayer of surrender in Gethsemane: "Not as I will, but as you will."

Catechism References

  • CCC 2626 – Prayer can take the form of blessing and adoration, petition, intercession, thanksgiving, and praise.
  • CCC 2700 – Vocal prayer uses our bodies and voices to express the inner prayer of the heart.
  • CCC 2705 – Meditation engages thought, imagination, emotion, and desire.
  • CCC 2709 – Contemplative prayer is a gaze of faith fixed on Jesus, a silent love.
  • CCC 2644 – The Holy Spirit inspires all forms of prayer in the Church.

Proclamation

"Prayer takes many forms, but all true prayer leads us into the heart of God."

Explanation

Prayer in the Catholic tradition is rich and diverse. It can be expressed in different ways:

  • Vocal Prayer – Spoken aloud or silently, using set prayers or spontaneous words.
  • Meditation – Reflecting deeply on God’s Word, truths of the faith, or His works.
  • Contemplation – Silent, loving presence before God, beyond words.

The Church also recognizes traditional types of prayer:

  • Adoration – Worship and praise of God for His greatness and holiness.
  • Contrition – Sorrow for sin and the desire for God’s mercy.
  • Thanksgiving – Gratitude for God’s blessings and grace.
  • Supplication (Petition) – Asking God for our needs and the needs of others.

Prayer can be personal or communal, liturgical or private, spontaneous or formal. The important thing is that it is sincere, rooted in faith, and open to the movement of the Holy Spirit.

By practicing various forms and types of prayer, we grow in relationship with God and learn to live in His presence at all times.

lessons.title: The Prayer of the Mass
lessons.contentHTML:
Definition

The Mass is the highest form of prayer in the Catholic Church, the source and summit of the Christian life. In it, the faithful unite themselves with Christ’s perfect offering to the Father through the power of the Holy Spirit (CCC 1324, 1325, 1348–1355).

Scriptural References

  • Luke 22:19–20 – "Do this in remembrance of me."
  • Acts 2:42 – "They devoted themselves to the apostles’ teaching and fellowship, to the breaking of bread and the prayers."
  • Hebrews 10:19–22 – We have confidence to enter the sanctuary by the blood of Jesus.
  • 1 Corinthians 11:23–26 – St. Paul recounts the institution of the Eucharist.

Catechism References

  • CCC 1324 – The Eucharist is the source and summit of the Christian life.
  • CCC 1325 – In the Eucharist, the unity of the faithful is both expressed and brought about.
  • CCC 1348 – The Mass is the gathering of the People of God, presided over by Christ Himself.
  • CCC 1350 – The offertory expresses our desire to offer ourselves with Christ.
  • CCC 1355 – Holy Communion unites us with Christ and with each other.

Proclamation

"In the Mass, heaven touches earth, and we are drawn into the eternal prayer of Christ to the Father."

Explanation

The Mass is the Church’s greatest prayer because it is Christ’s own sacrifice made present for us. It is both a memorial of His passion, death, and resurrection, and a real participation in that sacrifice.

The structure of the Mass includes:

  • Introductory Rites – Gathering, penitential act, and opening prayer.
  • Liturgy of the Word – Readings from Scripture, homily, Creed, and intercessions.
  • Liturgy of the Eucharist – Presentation of gifts, Eucharistic Prayer, and consecration.
  • Communion Rite – The Lord’s Prayer, sign of peace, and reception of Holy Communion.
  • Concluding Rites – Blessing and dismissal.

In the Mass, the faithful join in Christ’s eternal offering to the Father, uniting their prayers, works, joys, and sufferings with His perfect sacrifice. Participation in the Mass is both a duty and a privilege for Catholics, nourishing us with God’s Word and the Eucharist.

The Mass is not simply a ritual but an encounter with the living God. Through it, we are transformed, strengthened in grace, and sent forth to live the Gospel in the world.

lessons.title: Prayer Without Ceasing
lessons.contentHTML:
Definition

Prayer without ceasing is the continual orientation of our hearts toward God, living in His presence at all times, and offering every moment as an act of love and communion with Him (CCC 2565, 2742–2745).

Scriptural References

  • 1 Thessalonians 5:16–18 – "Rejoice always, pray without ceasing, give thanks in all circumstances."
  • Colossians 4:2 – "Continue steadfastly in prayer, being watchful in it with thanksgiving."
  • Romans 12:12 – "Rejoice in hope, be patient in tribulation, be constant in prayer."
  • Psalm 16:8 – "I keep the Lord always before me; because he is at my right hand, I shall not be moved."

Catechism References

  • CCC 2565 – Prayer is the living relationship of the children of God with the Holy Trinity.
  • CCC 2742 – "Pray constantly" is not limited to vocal prayer but is a habit of being in the presence of God.
  • CCC 2743 – Prayer and Christian life are inseparable; it is always possible to pray.
  • CCC 2744 – Prayer is a vital necessity; if we do not pray, we risk spiritual death.
  • CCC 2745 – Prayer animates the whole of the Christian life.

Proclamation

"To pray without ceasing is to live every moment in the awareness of God’s love and to respond with love."

Explanation

Praying without ceasing does not mean speaking prayers every moment, but living in a constant state of union with God. It is an attitude of the heart that keeps us mindful of His presence in all we do.

This continuous prayer is nourished by regular times of formal prayer and flows into daily life — in work, rest, relationships, and even struggles. Every action, when offered to God in love, becomes prayer.

Saints throughout history, such as St. Paul, the Desert Fathers, and St. Thérèse of Lisieux, lived this way, showing that every moment can be sanctified.

Practicing prayer without ceasing includes: frequent short prayers (aspirations), the habit of thanksgiving, turning to God in every decision, and offering up joys and sufferings for His glory.

This way of life transforms ordinary days into a continuous act of worship and prepares us for eternal communion with God in heaven.
